---
title: "Allergic sensitization to foods in India and other Low–Middle-income countries"
author: "Padukudru Anand Mahesh, Mohammed Kaleem Ullah, Ashwaghosha Parthasarathi"
year: 2023
doi: "10.1111/cea.14300"
journal: "Clinical & Experimental Allergy"
volume: "53(7):739–750"
pmid: "36825760"
access: "Paywalled — Wiley Online Library"
source_quality: "Abstract + PubMed summary only. Full text not accessed."
---

# Allergic sensitization to foods in India and other Low–Middle-income countries

**Authors:** Padukudru Anand Mahesh, Mohammed Kaleem Ullah, Ashwaghosha Parthasarathi
**Year:** 2023
**DOI:** 10.1111/cea.14300
**Journal:** Clinical & Experimental Allergy, 53(7):739–750
**PMID:** 36825760

## Why this paper

Brought in as the primary prevalence synthesis paper for Indian food allergy. Covers both sensitisation (immune system building a response to a food protein, detectable by blood or skin test) and clinical reactivity (actual symptoms when the food is eaten) — a distinction critical to this project. Also covers other low-middle-income countries (LMICs) for comparative context.

## Extracted points

> **Source quality note:** The following points are from PubMed abstract and search result summaries. Full text was paywalled and could not be accessed. Points marked [ABSTRACT] came from the abstract. Points marked [SEARCH SUMMARY] came from secondary search engine summaries — these are less reliable and should be verified against the full paper before being cited in the manuscript.

1. Food allergy prevalence in India (Karnataka): 0.14% in children, 1.2% in adults. — [SEARCH SUMMARY]
   Context: DON'T KNOW — study design, population size, how clinical food allergy was defined and confirmed.
   For us: This is the most-cited India-specific prevalence figure across multiple papers in our set. Low clinical prevalence despite high sensitisation is a recurring finding.

2. Allergic sensitisation to any food (serum-specific IgE): 26.5% in adults, 19.1% in children. — [SEARCH SUMMARY]
   Context: DON'T KNOW — population, region, which foods the IgE panel covered.
   For us: The gap between 19.1% sensitisation and 0.14% clinical prevalence in children is approximately 136-fold. This divergence is a key analytical point for the manuscript.

3. Allergic sensitisation to any food (skin prick test): 4.48% in children. — [SEARCH SUMMARY]
   Context: DON'T KNOW — population, which allergens were tested.
   For us: Skin prick test (a small amount of allergen solution placed on skin; a raised bump indicates sensitisation) and serum IgE give different sensitisation rates for the same population. The 4.48% vs 19.1% gap may reflect test sensitivity differences or panel coverage differences — cannot determine which without full text.

4. Serum-specific IgE sensitisation in children across 28 LMIC studies: 0.11%–16.8% range. — [SEARCH SUMMARY]
   Context: DON'T KNOW — which countries, which foods, what assay thresholds.
   For us: Wide range suggests methodological heterogeneity, not just genuine epidemiological variation.

5. Skin prick test sensitisation in children across LMICs: 0.14%–9.6% range. — [SEARCH SUMMARY]
   Context: DON'T KNOW — same caveats as above.

6. Allergens specifically highlighted as Indian: chickpea, eggplant. — [SEARCH SUMMARY]
   Context: DON'T KNOW — whether these were mentioned as most prevalent, most studied, or most distinctive compared to Western lists.
   For us: These two appear across multiple papers in our set as India-specific allergens not covered by the Big 8/9 or Big 14.

7. Authors call for international collaboration to standardise assessment approaches. — [ABSTRACT]
   For us: Relevant to the limitations section and the defensible list argument.

## Gaps and challenges the authors document

DON'T KNOW in detail — full text required to extract the specific evidence gaps the authors catalogue.
From search summary: methodological variation in questionnaires, skin testing materials, and allergen quantities tested; limited geographic coverage within India (beyond Karnataka).

## Questions for full text access

- What exact definition of "clinical food allergy" was used? (Oral food challenge? Physician diagnosis? Self-report + test confirmation?)
- What was the allergen panel used for the sensitisation testing?
- Which specific foods showed highest sensitisation rates in the Indian cohorts?
- Do the authors comment on the sensitisation–reactivity gap explicitly?
